diagnosis: The soft tissue inside your tooth (the pulp or nerve) has become infected or inflamed and will not heal on its own.
treatment_steps: Numb the area completely with local anesthesia
Isolate the tooth with a small protective sheet called a dental dam
Create a small access opening through the top of the tooth
Clean and disinfect the infected canal space using tiny instruments
Fill and seal the canal with a biocompatible material
Place a temporary or permanent filling; a crown is usually placed in a later visit
benefits: Saves your natural tooth instead of removing it
Treated teeth can last a lifetime with proper care and a crown
risks: Soreness, swelling, or bleeding after the procedure
Temporary or, rarely, permanent numbness or tingling in the tooth or surrounding tissue
Allergic reaction to injections or medications
Treatment failure requiring retreatment, minor surgery, or extraction
Reduced effectiveness of birth-control pills if certain antibiotics are prescribed
The tooth may become discolored over time
alternatives: Extraction with replacement options such as an implant, bridge, or partial denture
No treatment at this time (the infection will not resolve on its own and may worsen)
risks_of_declining: The infection will not resolve on its own. Left untreated it can lead to more pain, spreading infection to surrounding bone and tissue, and possible loss of the tooth.
prognosis: Generally good with high success rates when a crown is placed afterward. No outcome is guaranteed, but most root canals last many years with proper care.
